audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed as Bildad's second speech: rebuke of Job, trap/net imagery, light extinguished, terrors, disease/death imagery, uprooted memory, and fate of the wicked. Marked as Bildad's speech, not final theology.

verses.restored: Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
How long until you set an end to words? Understand, and afterward we will speak.
Why are we counted as beasts, considered stupid in your eyes?
You who tear yourself in your anger, shall the earth be forsaken for you, or rock move from its place?
Indeed, the light of the wicked is put out, and the flame of his fire does not shine.
Light grows dark in his tent, and his lamp above him is put out.
The steps of his strength are narrowed, and his own counsel throws him down.
For he is sent into a net by his own feet, and he walks on lattice-work.
A trap seizes him by the heel; a snare holds him fast.
A rope is hidden for him in the ground, and a trap for him on the path.
Terrors frighten him on every side and chase him at his heels.
His strength is hungry, and calamity is ready at his side.
It eats parts of his skin; the firstborn of death eats his limbs.
He is torn from his tent, his confidence, and marched to the king of terrors.
What is not his dwells in his tent; sulfur is scattered on his dwelling.
Below, his roots dry up; above, his branch withers.
His memory perishes from the earth, and he has no name on the face of the open land.
He is thrust from light into darkness and driven out of the world.
He has no offspring or descendant among his people, and no survivor where he sojourned.
Those in the west are appalled at his day, and those in the east are seized with horror.
Surely these are the dwellings of the unjust, and this is the place of one who does not know the Divine.
